headline: Surau Approvals Take 6 Months While Church Permits Average 4 Years
context: A 2025 comparison study by the Malaysian Consultative Council of Christianity found church building permits in Selangor averaged 4 years compared to 6 months for surau and mosques.

cards.big: Building a church in Selangor takes 4 years on average. A surau takes 6 months.
cards.sub: Both follow the same planning application process. The disparity is not in the law but in its application.

cards.big: Article 11(3)(a) guarantees the right to manage religious affairs.
cards.sub: This includes the right to establish places of worship — a right that is equal across religions.

cards.big: 47 church applications in Selangor have been pending for more than 5 years.
cards.sub: Common reasons for delay: 'awaiting resident feedback' and 'further study required.'

cards.big: Christians comprise 9.2% of Malaysia's population — 3 million people.
cards.sub: In Sabah and Sarawak, Christians are the majority. The disparity is primarily a Peninsular phenomenon.

cards.big: When the same law produces 6-month and 4-year timelines, the problem is not the law. It is the decision-maker.
Religious building permits should have statutory timelines and deemed-approval provisions — the same approach used for commercial development. Faith should not determine speed of approval.
